stops.name: Terminal Outpost
stops.subtitle: CLI Argument Parsing

code.content:
-- src/Cli.hs (simplified)
data Command
  = Store StoreCommand
  | Search Text [SearchAttribute] [Slug]
  | Login
  | Slots
  | Favorites FavoritesCommand
  | Basket BasketCommand
  | Checkout CheckoutCommand
  | Order OrderCommand
  | Suggestion SuggestionCommand
  | Categories
  deriving stock (Show, Eq)

commandParser :: Parser Command
commandParser =
  hsubparser $
    command "store" (info storeParser (progDesc "Show or set store"))
    <> command "search" (info searchParser (progDesc "Search products"))
    <> command "login" (info loginParser (progDesc "Authenticate"))
    <> command "basket" (info basketParser (progDesc "Manage basket"))
    <> command "checkout" (info checkoutParser (progDesc "Checkout"))
    <> command "orders" (info orderParser (progDesc "Manage orders"))

stops.narration: Every grocery run starts at the terminal. Korb uses optparse-applicative to define a sum type of all possible commands. Each variant carries its own data. The hsubparser combinator turns subcommands into a clean algebraic data type. No stringly-typed arguments here.

stops.name: Store Selection Pass
stops.subtitle: Finding Your Market

code.content:
-- src/StoreApi.hs
searchForStores :: HttpClient -> ZipCode -> IOE ApiError [PickupMarket]
searchForStores HttpClient{get} (ZipCode plz) = do
  res :: ReweResponse ServiceArea <-
    get (ApiUrl ("https://mobile-clients-api.rewe.de/api/service-portfolio/" <> plz)) [] []
  pure $ maybe [] (.pickupMarkets) res.data_.servicePortfolio

-- src/Storage.hs
data CurrentStore = CurrentStore
  { wwIdent :: WwIdent, zipCode :: ZipCode }

writeSettings :: WwIdent -> ZipCode -> IOE FileError CurrentStore
writeSettings (WwIdent wwIdent) (ZipCode zipCode) =
  liftIOE FileError $ do
    dir <- configDir
    createDirectoryIfMissing True dir
    TIO.writeFile (dir </> "selected_store") wwIdent
    TIO.writeFile (dir </> "selected_zip") zipCode
    pure $ CurrentStore (WwIdent wwIdent) (ZipCode zipCode)

stops.narration: Before shopping, you pick your local REWE market. The store API takes a zip code and returns pickup locations. Your selection gets persisted as plain text files in the XDG config directory. Simple, no database needed. This config is loaded on every subsequent command.

stops.name: Authentication Ridge
stops.subtitle: PKCE OAuth Flow

code.content:
-- src/Auth.hs
generatePKCE :: IO (ByteString, ByteString)
generatePKCE = do
  bytes <- getEntropy 32
  let verifier = encodeUnpadded bytes
      challenge = encodeUnpadded (convert (hash verifier :: Digest SHA256))
  pure (verifier, challenge)

loginFlow :: TokenStore -> HttpClient -> IOE AppError Text
loginFlow store HttpClient{urlFromEncodedPost} = do
  (url, verifier) <- liftE generateAuthUrl
  redirect <- liftIO $ do
    putStrLn "1. Open this URL in Chrome:"
    TIO.putStr url
    putStrLn "2. Log in normally (email, password, 2FA)"
    putStrLn "3. Copy the redirect URL and paste it here:"
    TIO.getLine
  code <- liftE $ except (extractCodeFromRedirect redirect)
  tknRes :: TokenResponse <-
    liftE $ urlFromEncodedPost (reweFormEncodedBody code verifier) tokenEndpoint
  liftE $ store.storeAccess tknRes.access_token
  liftE $ store.storeRefresh tknRes.refresh_token
  pure "Login succeeded, tokens stored"

stops.narration: The steepest climb on the trail. Korb reverse-engineers REWE's mobile app OAuth flow using PKCE. It generates a cryptographic verifier, hashes it with SHA-256 to create a challenge, and builds the authorization URL. The user logs in via browser, copies the redirect, and korb exchanges the auth code for tokens. Real crypto in a grocery CLI.

stops.name: Token Refresh Falls
stops.subtitle: JWT Validation and Refresh

code.content:
-- src/Auth.hs
decodeJWT :: AccessToken -> Either AuthError Int64
decodeJWT (AccessToken tkn) = do
  jwtPayload <- case splitOn "." tkn of
    (_ : token : _) -> first (AuthError . pack) $
      decodeUnpadded $ encodeUtf8 token
    _ -> Left $ AuthError "Access token in wrong format"
  case eitherDecodeStrict jwtPayload of
    Right (Exp expiry) -> pure expiry
    Left err -> Left (AuthError $ pack err)

getValidToken :: TokenStore -> HttpClient -> IOE AppError AccessToken
getValidToken store client = do
  token <- liftE store.readAccess
  expiry <- liftE $ except $ decodeJWT token
  now :: Int64 <- round <$> liftIO getPOSIXTime
  if now < expiry - 30 then pure token else refreshFlow store client

stops.narration: Crossing the Token Refresh Falls. Every authenticated request first checks if the JWT is still valid. Korb splits the JWT on dots, base64-decodes the payload, and extracts the expiry timestamp. If the token expires within 30 seconds, it silently refreshes using the stored refresh token. Users never see re-authentication.

stops.name: HTTP Client Gorge
stops.subtitle: mTLS and curl Wrapper

code.content:
-- src/HttpClient.hs
data HttpClient = HttpClient
  { get :: forall res. (FromJSON res) =>
      ApiUrl -> Headers -> QueryParams -> IOE ApiError res
  , post :: forall res req. (FromJSON res, ToJSON req) =>
      req -> ApiUrl -> Headers -> QueryParams -> IOE ApiError res
  , patch :: forall res req. (FromJSON res, ToJSON req) =>
      req -> ApiUrl -> Headers -> QueryParams -> IOE ApiError res
  , delete :: forall res. (FromJSON res) =>
      ApiUrl -> Headers -> QueryParams -> IOE ApiError res
  }

certPem :: ByteString
certPem = $(embedFile "certs/mobile-clients-api.rewe.de/private.pem")

mkHttpClient :: IOE AppError HttpClient
mkHttpClient = do
  (certPath, keyPath) <- liftE writeCertsToTemp
  curlBin <- liftE $ liftIOE FileError $
    pack . fromMaybe "curl" <$> lookupEnv "KORB_CURL"
  pure HttpClient { get = \url hdrs qps ->
    curlJson curlBin certPath keyPath GET url hdrs qps Nothing, ... }

stops.narration: Deep in HTTP Client Gorge, korb uses a record of polymorphic functions as its HTTP abstraction. The real trick is mTLS. Client certificates are extracted from the REWE mobile app and embedded into the binary at compile time with Template Haskell. They are written to temp files at runtime and passed to curl. No Haskell HTTP library needed.

stops.name: Product Search Prairie
stops.subtitle: Searching and Browsing

code.content:
-- src/ReweApi.hs
searchRewe :: RewePublicApi -> Text -> [SearchAttribute] -> [Slug]
  -> IOE ApiError [Product]
searchRewe RewePublicApi{search} query attributes categories = do
  let attributeFilters =
        (\a -> "attribute=" <> attributeToText a) <$> attributes
  let categoryFilters =
        (\(Slug slug) -> "categorySlug=" <> slug) <$> categories
  let filters = intercalate "&" (attributeFilters ++ categoryFilters)
  searchRes <- search [("query", query), ("filters", filters)]
  pure searchRes.data_.products.products

-- src/ReweApi/Types.hs
data Product = Product
  { articleId :: Text
  , productId :: ProductId
  , title :: Text
  , imageURL :: Text
  , orderLimit :: Maybe Int
  , listing :: Listing
  , attributes :: Maybe ProductAttributes
  }

stops.narration: Out on the Product Search Prairie, you find what you need. Filters for organic, vegan, regional, and vegetarian get converted to query parameters. Category slugs narrow the search further. The Product type captures everything REWE returns. Search is the only public endpoint that does not require authentication.

stops.name: Basket Management Camp
stops.subtitle: Cart Operations

code.content:
-- src/ReweApi.hs
basket :: ReweAuthedApi -> IOE ApiError Basket
basket ReweAuthedApi{getBaseket} = do
  reweRes <- getBaseket
  pure reweRes.data_.basket

basketsAdd :: ReweAuthedApi -> Item -> IOE ApiError (Maybe LineItem)
basketsAdd ReweAuthedApi{getBaseket, addItemToBasket}
  Item{listingId, quantity} = do
  reweResponse <- getBaseket
  let currentBasket = reweResponse.data_.basket
  reweRes <-
    addItemToBasket
      currentBasket.id
      listingId
      (fromMaybe (Qty 1) quantity)
      currentBasket.version
  let wasDelete = any (\(Qty q) -> q <= 0) quantity
  let addedLineItem =
        find (\li -> li.product.listing.listingId == listingId)
          reweRes.data_.basket.lineItems
  maybe
    (if wasDelete then pure Nothing
     else throwE $ ApiError "Item was not added to basket")
    pure $ Just addedLineItem

stops.narration: Setting up camp at Basket Management. Adding to the basket requires first fetching the current basket to get its ID and version, a form of optimistic concurrency control. Quantity zero means delete. The function checks if the item actually appeared in the response. Notice how Haskell's Maybe type makes the success and delete paths explicit.

stops.name: Checkout Frontier
stops.subtitle: Timeslot, Payment, and Order

code.content:
-- src/ReweApi.hs
checkoutTimeslot :: ReweAuthedApi -> TimeslotId
  -> IOE ApiError CheckoutResponse
checkoutTimeslot api@ReweAuthedApi{
  patchCheckoutTimeslot, reserveTimeslot = reserve, addPayment
  } timeslot = do
  co <- checkout api
  reservedTimeslot <- (.data_.createTimeslotReservation)
    <$> reserve timeslot
  _ <- (.data_) <$> patchCheckoutTimeslot
    co.basket.id co.checkout.id reservedTimeslot.slotId
  (.data_) <$> addPayment co.basket.id co.checkout.id

orderCheckout :: ReweAuthedApi -> IOE ApiError OrderResponse
orderCheckout api@ReweAuthedApi{postOrder, confirmOrder} = do
  co <- checkout api
  when (null co.checkout.timeslot) $
    throwE (ApiError "No timeslot for pickup set.")
  _ <- confirmOrder co.checkout.id co.basket.id
  (.data_) <$> postOrder co.checkout.id

stops.narration: You made it to Checkout Frontier! The final pipeline is a careful multi-step dance: create checkout from basket, reserve a timeslot, patch the timeslot onto the checkout, set payment to in-market, confirm the order, then place it. Each step is a separate API call. Miss any step and the order fails. Your wagon has arrived at the REWE pickup counter.

events.title: Cloudflare Storm!
events.text: A storm of TLS fingerprinting hits your requests! REWE's Cloudflare protection is blocking automated clients. How do you weather this storm?

choices.text: Use mTLS client certificates extracted from the mobile app
choices.explanation: Korb embeds the REWE mobile app's client certificates and presents them via curl's --cert and --key flags. This makes requests look like they come from the official app.

choices.text: Set a User-Agent header to pretend to be a browser
choices.explanation: User-Agent spoofing alone won't fool mTLS verification. The server requires a valid client certificate signed by the expected CA.

choices.text: Use a headless browser to make all API calls
choices.explanation: Running a full browser for JSON API calls is massive overkill. The mTLS certificates are the key that unlocks the mobile API.

events.title: A Haskell Developer Appears
events.text: A fellow traveler asks: 'Korb uses ExceptT e IO a everywhere, aliased as IOE. Why not just use IO with exceptions?'

choices.text: ExceptT makes errors visible in the type signature so the compiler enforces handling
choices.explanation: IOE ApiError forces callers to handle or propagate the specific error type. The LiftError typeclass then lets you compose different error domains into AppError. The compiler catches unhandled cases.

choices.text: ExceptT is faster than IO exceptions at runtime
choices.explanation: ExceptT is actually slightly slower due to the Either wrapper. The benefit is type safety, not performance.

choices.text: IO exceptions don't work with async code
choices.explanation: IO exceptions work fine with async code. The choice of ExceptT is about making error handling explicit in types, not about async compatibility.

events.title: The PKCE River
events.text: You've reached the deepest river on the trail. The OAuth flow requires proving you're the same client who started the authorization request. How do you cross?

choices.text: Generate a random verifier, hash it as a challenge, send the challenge first, then prove the verifier later
choices.explanation: PKCE generates a random verifier, SHA-256 hashes it into a code_challenge sent with the auth request, then sends the original verifier when exchanging the code. The server hashes the verifier to verify it matches. No client secret needed.

choices.text: Store a client secret in the binary and send it with every request
choices.explanation: Client secrets in distributed binaries are easily extracted. PKCE was invented specifically because native apps can't keep secrets. The challenge-verifier pattern proves identity without shared secrets.

choices.text: Use basic HTTP authentication with username and password
choices.explanation: Sending raw credentials with every request is insecure and doesn't support the redirect-based OAuth flow. PKCE provides cryptographic proof of the authorization initiator.

events.title: Expired Token Breakdown!
events.text: Disaster! Your access token expired mid-request. The basket API returns 401 Unauthorized. Your shopping list is still full!

choices.text: Check the JWT expiry before each request and silently refresh using the refresh token
choices.explanation: getValidToken decodes the JWT payload, checks if now < expiry - 30, and triggers refreshFlow if needed. The 30-second buffer prevents edge-case expiry during the request. Users never see auth errors.

choices.text: Catch the 401 error and ask the user to run korb login again
choices.explanation: Forcing re-login on every token expiry is terrible UX. The whole point of refresh tokens is transparent re-authentication without user interaction.

choices.text: Request a token with a very long expiry so it never expires
choices.explanation: Token lifetime is controlled by the server, not the client. Short-lived access tokens with refresh tokens is the secure pattern. Korb can't change REWE's token policy.

events.title: Type Storm!
events.text: A blizzard of API responses is hitting your code. REWE wraps every response in {"data": ...} and each endpoint returns different nested structures. How do you stay type-safe?

choices.text: Define a generic ReweResponse wrapper with GHC Generics-derived JSON parsing
choices.explanation: ReweResponse maps the 'data' field to 'data_' via fieldLabelModifier in genericParseJSON. Each endpoint gets its own response type inside. The compiler ensures you access only fields that exist.

choices.text: Parse everything as a raw JSON Value and use dynamic lookups
choices.explanation: Dynamic JSON access throws away all type safety. You'd discover missing fields at runtime instead of compile time. Aeson's FromJSON instances catch these errors early.

choices.text: Write manual JSON parsing for each response type
choices.explanation: Manual parsing is error-prone and verbose. GHC Generics derives correct FromJSON instances automatically from the Haskell record structure. Less code, fewer bugs.

events.title: A Smart Shopper Appears
events.text: A clever traveler shows you their workflow: 'I use korb suggestion threshold 5 to find items that push my cart past the free pickup minimum. It checks my last 10 orders and ranks by purchase frequency.' What's the key insight?

choices.text: Sort by purchase frequency descending, exclude items already in basket, take N
choices.explanation: The suggestion engine counts how often each product appears across recent orders, filters out items already in the basket, sorts by frequency descending, and takes the requested number. Formally verified with Lean 4 proofs.

choices.text: Recommend the cheapest items to minimize spending while hitting the threshold
choices.explanation: Price optimization misses the point. The suggestions should be items you actually want to buy. Frequency ranking ensures you get items you regularly purchase.

choices.text: Randomly select from past purchases to add variety
choices.explanation: Random selection would suggest items you rarely buy. Frequency ranking surfaces your staples, the items most likely to be useful additions.

events.title: Empty Basket Crash!
events.text: You try to checkout but the basket is empty! The API will reject an empty checkout and your order flow crashes.

choices.text: Check if lineItems is empty before calling the checkout API and throw a descriptive error
choices.explanation: Korb checks 'when (null currentBasket.lineItems)' and throws a clear ApiError before hitting the network. Fail fast with a good message beats a cryptic API error.

choices.text: Let the API return an error and display whatever it says
choices.explanation: API error messages are often cryptic and unhelpful. Client-side validation gives users actionable feedback before wasting a network round trip.

choices.text: Automatically add a suggested item to the basket so it's never empty
choices.explanation: Silently adding items the user didn't request would be surprising and potentially costly. The correct approach is to inform the user and let them decide.

deathMessages: You have died of expired access tokens.
You have died of mTLS certificate mismatch.
You have died of empty basket syndrome.
You have drowned in the PKCE River.
You have died of unhandled ExceptT errors.
You have died of Cloudflare TLS fingerprinting.
You have died of missing store configuration.
You have died of optimistic concurrency conflicts.
You have died of checkout without a timeslot.
You have starved waiting for the REWE API response.

sourceInfo.description: REWE delivery CLI - a Haskell CLI for ordering groceries via reverse-engineered REWE API
